title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — June 29, 2026
headline: Iran struck Kuwait and Bahrain June 28 — zero score changes; attack harm is Iran's conduct, not the targets'.
summary: Iran fired missiles and drones at the US military base in Kuwait and the US Fifth Fleet headquarters in Bahrain on June 28. Kuwait intercepted two ballistic missiles — no deaths, two minor shrapnel injuries. A drone strike in Muharraq, Bahrain damaged a home — no casualties. Neither government's score moves. The benchmark scores each government on what it does to its own people, not on what is done to it. Iran caused this harm and is scored accordingly. Kuwait holds at 25.0 of 100 in the Developing band. Bahrain holds at 9.4 of 100 in the Critical band. Sudan's president al-Burhan rejected the four-country Quad humanitarian ceasefire on June 29, vowing to fight until the RSF is dismantled. The RSF had accepted the proposal. The ceasefire is not in effect. More than 500,000 civilians remain besieged in El Obeid. Three rotation caches were corrected tonight. Kuwait was showing 20.0 instead of its live score of 25.0. Bahrain was showing 4.4 instead of 9.4. Qatar was showing 5.9 instead of 10.9. All three are now in line with the live index. Venezuela is now on Day 5 of the earthquake response. UNICEF's first 20-tonne shipment arrived June 27. The United States authorized 150 million dollars in relief funding. The score holds at 18.0 of 100. EU AI Act full applicability is 34 days away.

topSignals.title: Kuwait 25.0 and Bahrain 9.4 of 100: Iran Struck Both June 28 — Scores Unchanged; Harm Is Iran's Conduct, Not the Targets'
topSignals.whyItMatters: Iran launched missiles and drones at US military bases in Kuwait and Bahrain on June 28. No one died in either country. Neither government's score moves. The benchmark scores what a government does to its own people — not what is done to it.
topSignals.description: Kuwait holds at 25.0 of 100. Bahrain holds at 9.4 of 100. Iran fired ballistic missiles and drones at the US-run Ali Al Salem Air Base in Kuwait early on June 28. Kuwait's air defenses intercepted two of the ballistic missiles. No one died in Kuwait. Two people suffered minor shrapnel injuries. In Bahrain, Iran struck the US Fifth Fleet headquarters with missiles and drones. A drone damaged a home in Muharraq. Civil-defense crews responded. No one died. Amnesty International examined the Bahrain strikes and attributed responsibility to Iran, characterizing them as potential war crimes under international humanitarian law. Neither government's score moves because neither government caused this harm. The benchmark scores each government on its own conduct toward its own people — not on what is done to it by an external actor. Iran caused the harm and is scored accordingly. Only each government's own response conduct is examined for scoring purposes. That response record is consistent with each country's existing position. Kuwait's air defense and emergency response were effective — no internal crackdown occurred. That is consistent with Kuwait's 25.0 Developing score. Bahrain's civil-defense response was competent — no casualties. That is consistent with Bahrain's 9.4 Critical score. Kuwait's 25.0 already prices its chronic governance profile: welfare-state responsiveness offset by the stateless bidoon population of approximately 100,000 who are denied citizenship and rights, kafala-style migrant-labor vulnerability, and limited political pluralism. Bahrain's 9.4 already prices its own documented repression record: the 2011-uprising legacy, ongoing political prisoners, citizenship-stripping, and systemic discrimination against the Shia-majority population. Two screening rules blocked score changes in both countries. The attribution rule is the decisive one: Iran caused the harm, so it is scored against Iran. The directionality rule blocked an upgrade in both countries — each government surfaced on a negative-theater event (an attack), and competent air defense or civil response is not strong enough countervailing compassion conduct to drive an upgrade. The double-count rule blocked a downgrade — no new deliberate harm by either government cleared the five-point threshold. Three stale rotation caches were corrected this cycle: Kuwait was showing 20.0 instead of 25.0, Bahrain was showing 4.4 instead of 9.4, and Qatar was showing 5.9 instead of 10.9. All corrections used the authoritative live index. Watch flags are now open: for Kuwait, the Equity dimension (EQU — equal emergency protection for stateless bidoon and migrant workers); for Bahrain, Equity and Accountability (EQU, ACC — Shia-majority treatment under wartime emergency powers).

evidence.claim: Al Jazeera June 28: Iran attacked Kuwait and Bahrain with missiles and drones; Kuwait intercepted two ballistic missiles at Ali Al Salem Air Base with no fatalities; two minor shrapnel injuries; Bahrain drone hit Muharraq home with no casualties; both attacks attributed to Iran's conduct, not the target governments'.

Amnesty International June 2026: Iran's drone and missile strikes on Bahrain and Saudi Arabia may constitute war crimes; research attributes responsibility for Gulf-state harm to Iran under international humanitarian law, not the governments of the attacked states.

PBS June 28: Iran attacks Bahrain and Kuwait following US strikes; threat to halt talks issued; Kuwait intercepted two missiles; Bahrain civil defense responded to Muharraq drone impact; no deaths in either country.

topSignals.title: Sudan 0 of 100: al-Burhan Rejects Quad Ceasefire June 29 — Vows to Fight On; El Obeid Siege Day 17-Plus
topSignals.whyItMatters: Sudan's army chief rejected a four-country humanitarian ceasefire offer on June 29, vowing to fight until the RSF is dismantled. The RSF had accepted the proposal. More than 500,000 civilians are still trapped in El Obeid. Sudan has been at the lowest possible score for over a year.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds at 0 of 100. General al-Burhan, head of the Sudanese Armed Forces, rejected the Quad humanitarian ceasefire proposal on June 29, vowing to fight 'with the precision of digging with a needle' until the RSF is dismantled. The RSF had previously accepted the same proposal. The ceasefire is unimplemented. Al-Burhan's rejection is own-conduct reinforcing the floor — a governing authority actively refusing a humanitarian ceasefire is negative own-conduct. But Sudan already sits at the absolute floor; no lower score is possible. The RSF's acceptance of the Quad proposal becomes the current forward trigger: if the RSF were to observe a unilateral ceasefire in El Obeid while the SAF rejects one, that creates a complex attribution and scoring question for a future cycle. El Obeid has been under RSF drone siege for 17-plus days. More than 500,000 civilians remain in the city. Hospitals are offline. Famine has been declared in El-Fasher and Kadugli. UN Under-Secretary-General DiCarlo said on June 27 that the window to prevent mass atrocities in El Obeid is 'rapidly narrowing.' The RSF's documented pattern — drone strikes followed by a direct ground assault — killed roughly 6,000 civilians in El-Fasher in three days in May 2025. The Sudanese Armed Forces reopened the Dalang-Kadugli road on June 28 through a counterattack. That is an armed-operations move, not humanitarian conduct — it serves both sides' logistics in a civil war and has not produced civilian protection.

evidence.claim: Al Jazeera June 28-29: al-Burhan rejects ceasefire, vows to dismantle RSF; El Obeid 500,000-plus besieged; al-Burhan uses 'precision of digging with a needle' metaphor for sustained offensive.
UN News June 27: USG DiCarlo warns window to prevent mass atrocities in El Obeid is 'rapidly narrowing'; 500,000-plus civilians besieged; UNSC action demanded; no enforcement resolution issued.

topSignals.title: Iran 2.5 of 100: Gulf Strikes Are Iran's Own Negative Conduct — Near-Floor Reinforced; Doha Talks Are Tactical, Not Compassion Conduct
topSignals.whyItMatters: Iran's June 28 missile and drone strikes on Kuwait and Bahrain are Iran's own negative conduct — not the targets'. Combined with 784 or more executions this year at a 37-year high, Iran sits near the bottom at 2.5 of 100. The strikes do not clear a new threshold toward the absolute floor.
topSignals.description: Iran holds at 2.5 of 100. Unlike Kuwait and Bahrain — where Iran's strikes are attributed to Iran and scored against Iran — Iran's own score is directly affected by its conduct. The June 28 missile and drone attacks on Kuwait and Bahrain, and on Al Udeid in Qatar, are Iran's own negative conduct. Amnesty International characterizes the Gulf strikes as potential war crimes. But Iran already sits near the absolute floor at 2.5, and the strikes targeted US military bases with limited civilian casualties. They are a continuation of the aggression-plus-execution pattern that the 2.5 already prices. A near-floor score limits how much new negative conduct can depress the composite further: the strikes do not constitute deliberate mass civilian harm at a scale that clears the five-point threshold toward 0.0. Iran has conducted 784 or more executions year-to-date as of June 28 — a 37-year high. The execution rate continued through the Islamabad Memorandum period (June 14-17) without pause. Hunger strikes are in their 126th consecutive week across 56 prisons. Standing down from strikes or participating in Doha talks is tactical de-escalation under pressure — it is not positive compassion conduct toward Iran's own population. No upgrade. Confirm at 2.5; confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Al Jazeera June 28: Iran attacked Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar with missiles and drones; strikes targeted US military installations; Amnesty characterizes Gulf strikes as potential war crimes under international humanitarian law; Doha talks scheduled June 30.

NCRI June 28: 784-plus executions year-to-date in Iran — a 37-year high; execution rate continued without pause through the Islamabad Memorandum period; 126th consecutive week of hunger strikes across 56 prisons.

topSignals.title: Venezuela 18.0 of 100: Day 5 Earthquake Response — UNICEF Airlift Underway; US Authorizes 150 Million Dollars in Relief; Score Holds
topSignals.whyItMatters: UNICEF's first 20-tonne supply shipment arrived in Venezuela on June 27. The United States authorized 150 million dollars in relief funding. The score holds at 18.0 of 100. Aid is flowing in, but domestic delivery is still slow and access-restricted.
topSignals.description: Venezuela holds at 18.0 of 100. Day 5 of the earthquake response cycle. The June 24 twin magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 earthquakes killed approximately 1,450 people and injured 3,150. UNICEF's first 20-tonne supply shipment arrived June 27; a second 48-tonne shipment is due June 30. The United States Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) issued General License 60 on June 25, authorizing relief transactions through October 23, 2026 and committing 150 million dollars through UN and faith-based channels. These are new positive evidence items that reinforce the aid-acceptance finding from June 28. The government is not blocking international aid at the source. That remains what separates Venezuela from countries at the absolute floor — countries like Yemen, where the governing authority forced the World Food Programme out of all 365 northern-area staff positions. On the negative side: domestic relief remains slow, disjointed, and opaque. Field medical workers still cannot confirm what supplies have arrived. About 14,000 troops and police limit access with special permit requirements. Both screening rules from June 28 apply again today. The directionality rule blocks an upgrade: the entity surfaced on a negative event, and aid acceptance — even with new UNICEF and US evidence — is mild positive conduct, not strong enough to clear the upgrade bar. The double-count rule blocks a downgrade: the published score of 18.0 already accounts for Venezuela's weak institutional capacity and poor accountability. Watch flags on Action (ACT — relief responsiveness) and Accountability (ACC — aid-delivery transparency) remain open. The scored change triggers: documented deliberate aid obstruction or diversion is the downgrade trigger; demonstrably effective, transparent, and sustained relief and reconstruction delivery is the upgrade trigger. The 14-day reassessment window runs through approximately July 8.

evidence.claim: UNICEF USA June 2026: UNICEF's first 20-tonne supply shipment arrived in Venezuela June 27; a second 48-tonne shipment is due June 30; UNICEF calls for emergency support for children affected by the earthquakes.

US State Department June 25, 2026: OFAC General License 60 authorizes Venezuela earthquake relief transactions through October 23, 2026; 150-million-dollar commitment through UN and faith-based channels.
CNN June 28: survivors still being found in rubble Day 5; accountability concerns ongoing; government response described as disjointed by witnesses.

topSignals.title: Israel 0 of 100: Lebanon Strikes Continue Day 2 Post-Framework Collapse — UN COI Genocide Finding Remains on the Record
topSignals.whyItMatters: Israel has been striking Lebanon for a second consecutive day since the ceasefire framework collapsed June 28. The UN Commission of Inquiry's genocide finding from June 23 remains on the record. Israel is at the lowest possible score. No lower score exists.
topSignals.description: Israel holds at 0 of 100. Israel's air strikes on Lebanon continue into a second day following the collapse of the Rubio ceasefire framework. The framework was signed June 26, rejected by Hezbollah June 27, and effectively dead by June 28 when Israel resumed strikes. The strikes on June 29 are own-conduct reinforcing the floor — they are Israel's own actions. In Gaza, the UN Commission of Inquiry (report A/HRC/62/CRP.2, June 23) found that Israel continues to commit genocide and other atrocity crimes by deliberately targeting Palestinian children. Approximately 20,000 Palestinian children have been killed and 44,000 injured since October 2023. The Gaza ceasefire remains fragile — 936 fatalities since the October 2025 ceasefire agreement. The Kerem Shalom crossing is congested and the Zikim crossing has been closed for two weeks. The Lebanon forward triggers have been reset: a new ceasefire agreement and Lebanese Armed Forces assuming sovereign control of southern Lebanese territory and delivering reconstruction together would constitute scored positive own-conduct for Lebanon. Israel's floor score of 0 of 100 — the lowest in the index — reflects the most severe designation. No lower value is possible.

evidence.claim: Al Jazeera June 23 reporting on UN COI report A/HRC/62/CRP.2: Israel's deliberate targeting of children constitutes genocide and atrocity crimes; approximately 20,000 Palestinian children killed, 44,000 injured since October 2023.

boundaryWatch.trigger: EU AI Act full applicability on August 2 — 34 days away. Next US Commerce or BIS action on the Fable 5 export-control order. Mythos 5 partially unblocked June 26 for approximately 100 US companies and agencies.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary at 60.0. Ninth consecutive cycle in this range. Day 18 of the export-control suspension. Fable 5 remains fully suspended globally. EU AI Act applies August 2 — 34 days from tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: NLRB review of the unfair-labor-practice charge filed after Apple closed its Towson, Maryland store — its first unionized store. An NLRB complaint or ruling is the conversion trigger.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits 0.6 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary at 60.0. The Congressional Labor Caucus call for an NLRB investigation remains pre-adjudication. Fifth boundary-watch cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to bidoon or migrant workers during wartime — Equity downgrade trigger. Durable verified expansion of bidoon rights or citizenship recognition — upgrade trigger.
boundaryWatch.note: Kuwait at 25.0 Developing sits 5.0 points above the Critical-to-Developing boundary. EQU watch flag activated this cycle on bidoon and migrant-worker emergency-protection performance following Iran's June 28 attack. Day 1 of boundary watch.

boundaryWatch.trigger: July natural Ebola peak window — now active. M23 territory overlap with WHO response corridors in North Kivu is the floor-crossing trigger if documented health-response obstruction occurs.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC at 2.3 Critical through cycle 16 of the Ebola emergency. 1,155 cases and 304 deaths as of June 25. Ituri province accounts for 1,054 of 1,155 cases. Contact tracing at 45 percent. July peak window is active.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa. Both districts at IPC AMN Phase 5 — extreme acute malnutrition. End-of-June declaration window passed without issuance; famine risk window extends through September 2026.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia at 4.7 Critical. Buur Hakaba and Baidoa both at IPC AMN Phase 5. The end-of-June threshold date passed without a formal declaration. Famine risk window extends through September 2026. Sixth cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Coordinator-level Sahel-band calibration review. Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) was reassessed in June on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct. Mali's starting-point score of 12.5 is above the likely calibrated range.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali at 12.5 Critical on a starting-point seed score. The Sahel calibration flag is in its sixth consecutive day. The gap with Burkina Faso (6.3, reweighted June 1 on active-state-perpetration conduct) persists. Coordinator review covers Mali, Niger, and potentially Chad. No timeline set.

boundaryWatch.trigger: DOJ False Claims Act complaint (kickbacks and disability discrimination) and Barrows v. Humana class action (nH Predict AI claim denials). Either concluded finding confirms the Developing-band designation.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana at 35.2 Developing, 4.8 points below the Functional boundary. Eighth cycle post-downgrade. Not assessed this cycle. Neither the DOJ complaint nor the Barrows class action has a fixed hearing date.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Bengali Muslim and Rohingya expulsions documented in June 2026; detect-delete-deport digital surveillance continuing. Not assessed this cycle.
boundaryWatch.note: India at 15.6 Critical sits 0.6 points above the Critical floor through a fifteenth consecutive monitoring cycle. Not assessed tonight.

emergingRisks.risk: Kuwait's EQU watch flag is now open on bidoon and migrant-worker emergency protection. Kuwait's air-defense and civil response during Iran's June 28 attack did not mention its approximately 100,000 stateless bidoon or its large migrant-worker population in emergency-response documentation.
emergingRisks.detail: A documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to stateless or migrant populations during wartime would be scored negative own-conduct and a downgrade trigger from 25.0 of 100. Conversely, a documented equal emergency-protection response that explicitly extends to bidoon and migrant workers would be scored positive own-conduct. The watch flag runs through the current wartime conditions.

emergingRisks.risk: Bahrain's EQU and ACC watch flags are now open on Shia-majority wartime emergency powers. Whether wartime emergency conditions produce discriminatory conduct toward Bahrain's Shia-majority population or political prisoners is the open scored question.
emergingRisks.detail: Bahrain at 9.4 Critical already prices 2011-uprising legacy repression. A documented wartime crackdown, mass detention, or discriminatory denial of protection would be negative own-conduct — a further downgrade. Genuine release of political prisoners or restoration of civil and political rights would be the upgrade trigger. The watch flags track wartime emergency conduct.

emergingRisks.risk: Sudan's El Obeid siege is at its highest risk point since the June 20 UNSC warning. Al-Burhan's June 29 ceasefire rejection closes the near-term SAF-side diplomatic path. The RSF's drone-strikes-then-direct-assault pattern killed roughly 6,000 civilians in El-Fasher in three days in May 2025.
emergingRisks.detail: More than 500,000 civilians remain besieged in El Obeid. Day 17-plus of the RSF drone campaign. Hospitals are offline. UN Under-Secretary-General DiCarlo described the window as 'rapidly narrowing' on June 27. Al-Burhan's rejection means a SAF-side implemented ceasefire is unlikely in the near term. The RSF's prior acceptance of the Quad proposal becomes the current forward trigger.

emergingRisks.risk: DRC Ebola is at the July natural peak window. 1,155 cases and 304 deaths as of June 25. Contact tracing at 45 percent — half the rate needed to stop transmission. No approved Bundibugyo vaccine.
emergingRisks.detail: More than 70 health workers are infected, degrading response capacity. M23-controlled territory overlaps with WHO response corridors in North Kivu. Documented obstruction by M23 would extend Rwanda's perpetrator attribution to a secondary pathway. The DRC government's contact-tracing failure remains the scored own-conduct driver.

emergingRisks.risk: Venezuela's earthquake response enters a 14-day window ending approximately July 8. That is the standard interval for distinguishing capacity failure from deliberate aid obstruction or diversion.
emergingRisks.detail: New UNICEF and US State Department evidence on Day 5 reinforces the aid-acceptance finding. Domestic delivery opacity and access restrictions remain. If diversion, deliberate obstruction, or casualty-data suppression is documented within the 14-day window, the ACT and ACC watch flags reach threshold — downgrade trigger. A demonstrably effective and transparent reconstruction effort would be the upgrade trigger.

emergingRisks.risk: Myanmar's aid-delivery collapse, documented in an OHCHR report dated June 22, has not yet been assessed in the rotation. If confirmed, this may be a scored change trigger for Myanmar's score.
emergingRisks.detail: The OHCHR June 22 report documents aid-delivery collapse in Myanmar. Myanmar was not in tonight's assessed top 15 and is logged for the next rotation cycle. A full assessment against Myanmar's published score would determine whether the OHCHR finding clears the threshold for a proposal.

emergingRisks.risk: The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable on August 2 — 34 days away. General-purpose AI transparency obligations, Article 50 labeling, and whistleblower protections activate. xAI and OpenAI have not published compliance roadmaps as of this cycle.
emergingRisks.detail: A post-applicability non-compliance finding for general-purpose AI models constitutes a Tier-5 methodological finding and would move scores. Anthropic's compliance infrastructure is documented. OpenAI's US-government-mandated restricted GPT-5.6 release (June 29, attributed to US government action) is noted for the next rotation cycle. The compliance gap between labs will become legally significant on August 2.

emergingRisks.risk: Somalia now has two districts at IPC AMN Phase 5 extreme acute malnutrition. The end-of-June declaration window passed without a formal IPC famine declaration. The risk window extends through September 2026.
emergingRisks.detail: WFP is reaching one in ten people who need food. Al-Shabaab continues to obstruct deliveries. The August lean-season peak will compound the situation. A formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration for Buur Hakaba or Baidoa is the scored change trigger for Somalia's 4.7-of-100 score.

emergingRisks.risk: Mali's enforced-disappearance and extrajudicial-killing pattern — documented by UN Special Procedures as potentially constituting crimes against humanity — remains unresolved in the Sahel calibration framework. The flag is in its sixth consecutive day.
emergingRisks.detail: The coordinator-level review covers Mali, Niger, and potentially Chad. Mali holds a starting-point score of 12.5 compared with Burkina Faso's reweighted 6.3. No timeline is set for the review. The gap has persisted through the entire June 2026 series.

sectorAlerts.sector: Countries — Gulf Attribution Screen Under Active Regional War
sectorAlerts.headline: Four Gulf states screened on the same attribution basis in one cycle — Iran's attacks on Kuwait and Bahrain show the benchmark operating under live multi-country war conditions.
observations: Kuwait (25.0 of 100), Bahrain (9.4 of 100), Qatar (10.9 of 100), and Saudi Arabia (9.4 of 100) all appeared in tonight's scan due to Iran's June 28 missile and drone attacks. All four screened on the attribution principle: Iran caused the harm; each target is scored on its own conduct only.

Effective air defense and civil response in Kuwait and Bahrain — no deaths in either country — is competent own-conduct consistent with their existing bands. It is not strong countervailing compassion conduct that drives an upgrade when surfaced on a negative-theater event.

Triple cache correction: Kuwait (20.0→25.0), Bahrain (4.4→9.4), Qatar (5.9→10.9) — all three drifted by exactly +5.0 points, indicating a synchronized prior-calibration event not propagated to the rotation cache. Corrected to live index values.

EQU watch flags open for Kuwait (bidoon and migrant-worker emergency protection) and Bahrain (Shia-majority treatment under wartime emergency powers). These are the conditions most likely to generate future proposals from the Gulf cluster.

sectorAlerts.sector: Countries — Sudan: Ceasefire Rejected; Siege Day 17-Plus
sectorAlerts.headline: Sudan's ceasefire arc shifted from 'SAF skeptical' to 'SAF rejected' in 24 hours — Al-Burhan vows to fight on; El Obeid at imminent risk.
observations: Al-Burhan rejected the Quad humanitarian ceasefire proposal June 29, describing the military campaign against the RSF as 'digging with a needle.' The RSF had accepted the proposal. The proposal is unimplemented.

The SAF rejection closes the near-term SAF-side diplomatic path. The RSF's acceptance now stands as the most concrete forward trigger: observed RSF unilateral restraint in El Obeid would generate a complex attribution question.

El Obeid: Day 17-plus RSF drone siege. 500,000-plus civilians. UN describes the window as 'rapidly narrowing.' The RSF's documented pattern — drone strikes then direct assault — killed roughly 6,000 civilians in El-Fasher in three days in May 2025.
Sudan remains at 0 of 100. Al-Burhan's rejection is own-conduct reinforcing the floor. No lower score is possible.

observations: Sudan (0 of 100): ceasefire rejected June 29; El Obeid siege Day 17-plus; famine in El-Fasher and Kadugli.
Israel (0 of 100): Lebanon strikes continue Day 2 post-framework collapse; UN COI genocide finding on record.
Yemen (0 of 100): Houthi-forced WFP termination of all 365 northern-area staff contracts continues across multiple cycles.
South Sudan (0 of 100): 7.8 million food insecure; 140,000 displaced Akobo; floor reinforced.
Iran (2.5 of 100): Gulf strikes are Iran's own negative conduct; 784-plus executions year-to-date (37-year high); near-floor reinforced without clearing a new threshold toward 0.
DRC (2.3 of 100): Ebola July peak window active; 1,155 cases, 304 deaths; contact tracing at 45 percent.
Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100): Sahel lean-season food crisis; Sahel calibration flag sixth day.

sectorAlerts.headline: UNICEF airlift and 150-million-dollar US relief license are the Day 5 evidence additions — both reinforce the aid-acceptance finding; ACT and ACC watch flags remain open.
observations: Venezuela (18.0 of 100): UNICEF's first 20-tonne supply shipment arrived June 27; second 48-tonne shipment due June 30. US OFAC General License 60 (June 25) authorizes 150 million dollars in relief through UN and faith-based channels.
Both are new positive evidence items that reinforce the June 28 determination: the government is not blocking aid at the source. That is what separates Venezuela from floor-cluster states.
Domestic relief remains slow, disjointed, and opaque. 14,000-plus troops and police limit access. Field medical workers cannot confirm what supplies have arrived.

The 14-day reassessment window runs through approximately July 8. Documented deliberate obstruction or diversion is the downgrade trigger. Sustained effective transparent relief is the upgrade trigger. ACT and ACC watch flags remain open.

methodologyNotes.name: Gulf-State Attribution Under Active Regional War — Four Entities, One Session, One Attribution Basis
methodologyNotes.description: June 29 is the first cycle in the benchmark's record where four entities simultaneously appeared in the scan because a single external actor attacked them and were screened under the same attribution principle. The protocol: (1) attribution — Iran caused the harm; scored against Iran, not the targets; (2) own-conduct screening for each target separately — Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Saudi Arabia evaluated independently; (3) directionality blocked upgrades in all four — each surfaced on a negative-theater event, and competent air defense or civil response is not strong countervailing compassion conduct; (4) double-count blocked downgrades in all four — no new deliberate harm by any of the four governments cleared the five-point threshold. The procedure was consistent across all four and produced identical outcomes: confirm at live baseline, delta 0.0. This is the highest-count simultaneous attribution screen in the June 2026 series.

methodologyNotes.name: Triple Cache Correction — Systematic Prior-Calibration Event Identified
methodologyNotes.description: Kuwait (20.0→25.0), Bahrain (4.4→9.4), and Qatar (5.9→10.9) each had rotation cache values drifting +5.0 points below their live index values. The uniform magnitude across all three Gulf entities suggests a coordinated prior-calibration event updated all three scores simultaneously, but the rotation cache was not refreshed at that time. The correction is straightforward — all three assessments used live index values from site/src/data/indexes/countries.json throughout and no score determinations were affected. Combined with the Venezuela correction (June 28), four entities across two geographic clusters (Gulf, Latin America) required cache corrections in consecutive cycles. The live index is the authoritative source; the rotation cache is a convenience index only.

forwardTriggers.trigger: Documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to stateless bidoon or migrant workers during wartime — Equity (EQU) downgrade trigger from 25.0 of 100. Durable and independently verified expansion of bidoon rights or citizenship recognition — EQU upgrade trigger.

Documented wartime crackdown, mass detention of Shia-majority population, or discriminatory denial of emergency protection — EQU and ACC downgrade trigger from 9.4 of 100. Genuine verified release of political prisoners or restoration of civil and political rights — upgrade trigger.

An implemented, civilian-protecting ceasefire with unobstructed aid access is scored positive own-conduct. RSF commencement of direct assault on El Obeid residential zones — follows the El-Fasher pattern — is the next downgrade-reinforcement event (though Sudan is already at the floor). Al-Burhan's June 29 rejection closes the near-term SAF-side ceasefire path.

14-day reassessment window from June 24 earthquake closes approximately July 8. Documented deliberate aid obstruction, diversion or theft of relief supplies, or suppression of casualty data — scored negative own-conduct, downgrade trigger from 18.0 of 100. Demonstrably effective, transparent, and sustained relief and reconstruction delivery — scored positive own-conduct, upgrade trigger. ACT and ACC watch flags open.

A new ceasefire agreement AND Lebanese Armed Forces (LAF) assuming sovereign control of southern Lebanese territory AND reconstruction delivery together constitute scored positive own-conduct. The Rubio framework arc is closed as of June 28. A new diplomatic process is required.

14-day reassessment checkpoint — standard interval for distinguishing capacity failure from deliberate obstruction. Evidence on ACT (relief responsiveness) and ACC (aid-delivery transparency) assessed at this date.
Full resolution-phase reassessment due. Conversion trigger: reversal of the June 8 codified-impunity law and release of detained union leaders.

July natural Bundibugyo Ebola peak window — now active. 1,155 cases and 304 deaths as of June 25. M23 territory overlap with WHO response corridors in North Kivu is the secondary trigger for attributing obstruction to Rwanda.
EU AI Act full applicability — general-purpose AI transparency obligations, Article 50 labeling, and whistleblower protections activate. No public compliance roadmap from xAI as of this cycle.

EU AI Act full applicability — same obligations apply. No public compliance roadmap from OpenAI as of this cycle. US-government-mandated restricted GPT-5.6 release (June 29) is US government conduct, not OpenAI own-conduct; logged for next rotation.

EU AI Act full applicability — 34 days away. Anthropic's compliance infrastructure is documented. Fable 5 remains suspended; Mythos 5 partially unblocked for US orgs. Day 18 of the export-control order.
August IPC lean-season update — IPC Phase 5 Borno trajectory and government response adequacy. Actual WFP food-assistance cuts and a realized Phase 5 famine declaration would be scored triggers.

Famine declaration window open through September 2026. Buur Hakaba and Baidoa both at IPC AMN Phase 5. End-of-June threshold date passed without formal issuance. Each new formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration in Somalia is a scored change trigger.

Whether OCHA or IOM can establish alternative food-delivery mechanisms in northern Yemen following WFP's forced termination of all 365 staff contracts. Without food-agency presence, delivery to 22.3 million people is severely constrained.

Coordinator-level Sahel-band calibration review — Mali (12.5 of 100) versus reweighted peer Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct. Flag in its sixth day; no timeline set.
42-state attorney general proceedings and Florida criminal investigation — no fixed hearing dates; any concluded finding, settlement, or criminal charge constitutes a scored change trigger.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: Kuwait's air defenses intercepted Iran's June 28 ballistic missiles with no fatalities — does that wartime civilian-protection result extend to Kuwait's approximately 100,000 stateless bidoon, and would documented exclusion of bidoon from emergency protection register on the Equity dimension?
dailyOpeningQuestion.tensionFraming: Kuwait's interception of Iran's June 28 missiles produced no fatalities for Kuwait's general population — but emergency-response documentation did not address Kuwait's approximately 100,000 stateless bidoon residents.
